sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A survey team developed a 12-item loneliness scale for college clinics. In 300 students, Cronbach’s alpha was 0.88. Two weeks later, 120 repeated the scale; test-retest correlation was 0.82. Scores correlated moderately with close contacts (r = -0.46) and strongly with an established loneliness inventory (r = 0.74). Students completing the scale in a waiting room scored higher than students completing it online at home. Several international students said some phrases were hard to interpret.

explanations.A: Criterion validity concerns relation to an outcome or criterion. Cronbach’s alpha specifically evaluates how well scale items hang together. The relevant evidence instead centers on scale item consistency.
explanations.B: Random assignment allocates participants to conditions by chance. The stem may ask about measurement or observational limitations rather than an experimental solution. The passage focus is scale item consistency, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Correct. The items appear to measure a common construct at the same time point. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.D: Negative reinforcement increases behavior by removing an aversive stimulus. The described effect does not involve removing an unpleasant condition after a response. The passage focus is scale item consistency, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Reliability and validity coefficients reported for the new 12-item loneliness scale, including internal consistency, test-retest reliability, and correlations with related measures.

explanations.A: A correlation of 0.82 is strong but imperfect. Measurement error or real short-term change can still exist. The relevant evidence instead centers on score stability.
explanations.B: A test-retest correlation measures stability of scores. It does not show that completing the loneliness scale caused loneliness to change. The relevant evidence instead centers on score stability.
explanations.C: Test-retest reliability evaluates stability of scores over time. It does not require every item on the scale to use identical wording. The relevant evidence instead centers on score stability.
explanations.D: Correct. The scale produces relatively stable scores over time. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.

questions.stem: Correlation with an established loneliness inventory supports:

explanations.A: Correct. The new scale correlates strongly with a measure of the same construct. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.B: Discriminant validity means weak relation with unrelated measures. The passage does not use an unrelated comparison measure. The passage focus is related loneliness measure, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.C: Intergenerational mobility is movement between social classes across generations. It is unrelated to validating a loneliness scale. The relevant evidence instead centers on related loneliness measure.
explanations.D: Sensory adaptation is reduced receptor responsiveness to constant stimulation. The item centers on cognition, social context, or decision processes instead. The passage focus is related loneliness measure, so this option is less directly supported.

questions.stem: Higher waiting-room scores raise concern about:

explanations.A: A setting-related score difference suggests measurement may not be invariant across contexts. It does not show perfect equivalence. The relevant evidence instead centers on survey setting effect.
explanations.B: Correct. The setting may influence responses independently of loneliness. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.C: A fixed-ratio schedule rewards behavior after a set number of responses. The passage does not describe a predictable response-count reward rule. The passage focus is survey setting effect, so this option is less directly supported.
explanations.D: Absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus detected about 50% of the time. The question is not about minimum sensory intensity. The passage focus is survey setting effect, so this option is less directly supported.

choices.D: Construct validity across cultural or language groups

explanations.A: Internal consistency can be high even when groups interpret items differently. The wording concern is about cross-group construct validity. The relevant evidence instead centers on item interpretation.
explanations.B: Numerical scores can be useful, but they must measure the same construct across language and cultural groups. The relevant evidence instead centers on item interpretation.
explanations.C: Random sampling affects representativeness. It does not by itself ensure item wording has the same meaning across cultural groups. The relevant evidence instead centers on item interpretation.
explanations.D: Correct. Different item interpretation can make scores noncomparable across groups. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.

questions.passage: A public health team studied whether a peer-mentoring program was associated with lower vaping among high school students. Schools chose whether to adopt the program. In adopting schools, 18% reported past-month vaping; in nonadopting schools, 26% did. Adopting schools also had smaller classes, more counselors, and higher parent volunteer participation. Students attending at least four mentor meetings had lower vaping rates than those attending none, but attendance was voluntary.
questions.stem: Why interpret the mentoring-vaping association cautiously?

choices.C: Schools self-selected and differed on resources.

explanations.A: Percentages are appropriate for comparing vaping rates. The problem is nonrandom program adoption and confounding. The relevant evidence instead centers on school-level self-selection.
explanations.B: Vaping can be studied ethically through protected surveys and public health designs. The main issue is causal inference, not impossibility. The relevant evidence instead centers on school-level self-selection.
explanations.C: Correct. Adoption was not random, and other school resources may explain lower vaping. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.D: A peer-based format could be useful. The design concern is voluntary school adoption and confounding resources. The relevant evidence instead centers on school-level self-selection.

questions.stem: Smaller classes and more counselors are:

explanations.A: Correct. They differ by adoption status and could independently lower vaping. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.B: The dependent variable is vaping. Class size, counselors, and parent volunteers are outside school features that could affect both program adoption and vaping. The relevant evidence instead centers on school resources.
explanations.C: Positive symptoms are added experiences such as hallucinations. School resources are institutional confounders, not psychotic symptoms. The relevant evidence instead centers on school resources.
explanations.D: Primary reinforcers satisfy biological needs. School counselors and class size are institutional resources, not innate rewards. The relevant evidence instead centers on school resources.

questions.stem: Why is voluntary meeting attendance limited evidence?

choices.B: Attendees may differ in motivation or baseline risk.

explanations.A: Mentor meetings could matter. The limitation is that voluntary attendance makes attendees systematically different from nonattendees. The relevant evidence instead centers on voluntary attendance.
explanations.B: Correct. Students who choose meetings may already be less likely to vape. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.C: Subgroup analyses can be informative, but self-selected subgroup membership can worsen bias. The relevant evidence instead centers on voluntary attendance.
explanations.D: Peer influence can matter greatly for adolescent vaping. The limitation is whether the voluntary program caused the lower rate. The relevant evidence instead centers on voluntary attendance.

questions.stem: Best design to test program effect?

choices.C: Randomly assign comparable schools to immediate or delayed program adoption.

explanations.A: Surveying only students who like mentoring would increase selection bias and reduce generalizability. The relevant evidence instead centers on testing program causality.
explanations.B: Ignoring school resources leaves confounding unaddressed. The passage states adopting schools differed in class size, counselors, and parent involvement. The relevant evidence instead centers on testing program causality.
explanations.C: Correct. Cluster randomization would better balance school-level confounders. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
explanations.D: Principal opinions do not measure the student behavior outcome. Testing program effectiveness requires measuring vaping under a stronger design. The relevant evidence instead centers on testing program causality.

choices.D: Reliable measurement and causal inference are separate requirements.

explanations.A: Reliability concerns consistency of measurement. It does not show that one variable causes another. The relevant evidence instead centers on measurement and design.
explanations.B: Observational studies can reveal associations and generate hypotheses. They are limited mainly when claims are overstated as causal. The relevant evidence instead centers on measurement and design.
explanations.C: Validity is central to psychology and sociology because constructs such as loneliness, stigma, and support must be measured accurately. The relevant evidence instead centers on measurement and design.
explanations.D: Correct. A scale can be reliable yet biased, and group differences can be noncausal when confounded. This answer uses the passage data rather than overextending beyond the study design.
